transcript: On a summer afternoon in July 2022, dispatch received a frantic 911 call, following the report of a reckless driver in Racine, Wisconsin. According to the 51-year-old caller, he was driving southbound on I-94, when a semi merged onto the highway, forcing him to quickly switch lanes. He believed his sudden maneuver may have triggered another driver, a 33-year-old woman, who then allegedly pointed a black handgun at him and sped off recklessly. The man continued to provide real-time updates about her location. With the possibility of an armed driver threatening other motorists, multiple Racine County Sheriff's deputies were immediately dispatched to intercept the vehicle before things could escalate any further.
Given the nature of the call and the report of a firearm, deputies conducted a high-risk traffic stop that required shutting down all southbound traffic for safety.

Deputies proceeded to search the vehicle and found an unloaded 9 mm handgun inside the glove box. In addition, they discovered a 9 mm magazine containing four live rounds inside a small backpack on the front passenger seat, within the driver's reach. At the jail, the woman told deputies the other driver had been the one driving aggressively while she was on the road with her two children. She admitted grabbing her firearm from the glove box and said she may have raised it above the door frame so the other driver could see it. However, she claimed the gun was unloaded, had no magazine inserted, and was never pointed at anyone, stating she only brandished it out of fear for her child's safety. When asked why she didn't call police if she felt threatened, she had no answer. She did provide deputies with video footage from her phone, which according to the report, showed the caller's vehicle was not being driven recklessly. Deputies also confirmed that she had no prior felony convictions and held a valid concealed carry permit. Arrangements were then made for her sister to take custody of the children.

As a result, the 33-year-old woman was charged with misdemeanor counts of intentionally point a firearm at a person, disorderly conduct, and she was eventually released on a $650 cash bond. Almost two and a half years later, she was found guilty of disorderly conduct after entering a no contest plea. The other charge was dismissed on the prosecutor's motion. Ultimately, she was sentenced to pay only a $200 fine.
